objective: Write four production-ready STAR stories covering the core influencing scenarios every APM faces, and complete a full mock behavioral interview scored on structure and specificity.

realWorldAnchor.context: Behavioral interviews at Indian product companies differ from US-style PM interviews in one key way: Indian hiring managers care deeply about how you handled real ambiguity in an Indian organisational context — founder-led cultures, flat hierarchies, engineering-heavy teams, and resource constraints that US case-study prep books do not cover. APM panels at Flipkart, Razorpay, and CRED consistently include behavioral questions about influencing without authority, data-driven decision-making under pushback, and cross-functional conflict resolution.

teamContext: All four teams (Engineering, Design, Data, Leadership) are relevant today — each STAR story covers a different cross-functional relationship that an APM must navigate from Day 1.

tasks.title: Behavioral interviews for PMs: what they test and how to prepare
tasks.summary: Behavioral interviews test the Influencing People pillar of the Reforge model — not your frameworks, but your judgment and interpersonal skill in past situations. This reading covers what hiring managers are actually evaluating (structured thinking, ownership, intellectual honesty, cross-functional maturity), how the STAR method works specifically for PM behavioral questions, and the most common failure modes: stories that are too vague, outcomes that are not quantified, and answers that describe the team's work rather than the candidate's specific contribution.
keyTakeaways: STAR stands for Situation, Task, Action, Result — but for PM interviews, the Action section is most heavily weighted because it reveals your judgment, not just what happened
The most common behavioral interview failure: saying 'we' throughout the answer — interviewers are evaluating what YOU specifically did, not what the team did collectively

Quantify results wherever possible — 'the feature launched' is a weak ending; 'the feature launched, drove 14% lift in checkout completion, and became the template for two subsequent features' is a strong ending

For influencing-without-authority stories, the structure is: what was the disagreement, what was your evidence, how did you bring the other person along, and what was the outcome for the product and the relationship
Indian hiring managers specifically probe for intellectual honesty — a story where you were wrong and updated your view is often more impressive than a story where you were right and prevailed

tasks.title: Influencing without authority: how PMs build alignment across engineering, design, and data
tasks.summary: A PM has no direct authority over any of the people whose work determines the product's success. The entire job is influence — through clarity of thought, strength of evidence, quality of relationships, and consistency of follow-through. This reading covers the specific influence tactics that work in Indian startup environments: pre-alignment before meetings, framing decisions in terms of the other person's goals, using data as a shared language rather than a weapon, and building credibility through small kept promises before making large asks.
keyTakeaways: Pre-alignment is the most underrated PM skill: the outcome of a product review meeting is usually decided in the one-on-one conversations that happen before it
Framing disagreements as shared problems — 'we both want the user to succeed, let me show you why I think this approach gets there faster' — is more effective than framing them as debates to win
Data as a shared language means bringing the engineer or designer into the data exploration together, not presenting data as a verdict; people support conclusions they helped reach
Credibility is built through the smallest interactions: if you say the spec will be ready Thursday, it must be ready Thursday — APMs who are reliable on small things get trusted with large decisions

Managing up is a form of influencing: giving your manager the minimum information they need to unblock you — not a status update, but a specific ask with a deadline — is what separates PMs who move fast from PMs who wait for decisions

tasks.keyword: APM product manager behavioral interview questions Flipkart Razorpay CRED Zepto India
tasks.title: Research real behavioral questions asked at Indian product companies
tasks.whyItMatters: Behavioral interview questions at Indian companies are shaped by specific organisational cultures. Knowing the actual questions Flipkart, Razorpay, and CRED ask — which are documented in Glassdoor, AmbitionBox, and product community forums — lets you prepare stories that address the real competencies these companies test for, not the generic US-PM-interview prep framework.
whatToLearn: What behavioral questions do Indian product companies (Flipkart, Razorpay, CRED, Swiggy, PhonePe) most commonly ask in APM/PM interviews?
What does 'influencing without authority' look like in a flat-hierarchy Indian startup vs a larger company like Flipkart or PhonePe?
What is the most common STAR story mistake that Indian APM candidates make in product interviews?
What competencies do Indian product hiring managers say they care most about that US PM prep books do not cover?
Are there documented examples of APM interview panels at Indian companies and what the rounds cover?

tasks.promptTitle: STAR story construction: build one strong behavioral story with a coach before the assessment
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM interviewer at an Indian product company conducting a mock behavioral interview.

Role: Help me build one strong STAR story before my full mock interview. I will pick one of the four scenarios I need to prepare, draft the story out loud or in text, and you will give me structured feedback before I do all four in my assessment.

Today I studied:
- What behavioral interviews test: judgment, ownership, cross-functional maturity
- STAR structure: Situation, Task, Action, Result — with Action weighted most heavily
- Influencing without authority: pre-alignment, data as shared language, credibility through reliability
- Real behavioral questions from Indian product companies

The four stories I need to write for my assessment:
1. A time I influenced without formal authority
2. A time I used data to make a decision someone disagreed with
3. A time I handled a disagreement with a designer or engineer
4. A time I shipped something under time pressure

Activity:
Step 1 — Pick one of the four stories above that feels most natural to you right now. Tell me which one.

Step 2 — Draft the story in full STAR format. Aim for 90-120 seconds of spoken length (roughly 200-250 words written). Include: specific context (company, team, timeline), what you specifically did (not 'we'), a quantified outcome, and one thing you learned.

Step 3 — I will give feedback on: Did you say 'we' too much? Is the Action section specific? Is the outcome quantified? Is the story relevant to an APM context? Does it show the competency it is supposed to show?

Step 4 — You will revise the story based on my feedback and submit the final version.

After feedback:
WHAT WORKED: [specific STAR elements done well]
WHAT MISSED: [specific gaps in structure, specificity, or outcome]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one note about how this story would land with an Indian product interviewer specifically]
COACHING NOTE: [one thing to apply to all four stories in the assessment]

Begin by asking me which of the four stories I want to practice.

bonusTasks.title: Search for AmbitionBox or Glassdoor APM interview reviews at Indian companies
bonusTasks.keyword: APM associate product manager interview experience Razorpay Zepto CRED Glassdoor AmbitionBox 2023 2024
bonusTasks.why: First-hand interview experience reports on AmbitionBox and Glassdoor give you actual question wordings and the structure of APM interview panels at Indian companies — more current and India-specific than any prep guide.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write four STAR stories covering the core APM behavioral scenarios, then run a full mock behavioral interview using the Claude prompt provided — answer all four questions, get scored on structure and specificity.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Part 1 — Write four STAR stories (20 minutes, 5 minutes each)

Write one STAR story for each of the following scenarios. Each story: 150-200 words. Must include a specific company/team/timeline context, what YOU specifically did (not 'we'), and a quantified or clearly stated outcome.

Story 1: A time you influenced a decision without having formal authority over the person you needed to influence.

Story 2: A time you used data to push for a decision that someone on your team or above you initially disagreed with.

Story 3: A time you had a significant disagreement with a designer or engineer about a product decision — what was the disagreement, what did you do, and what was the outcome?

Story 4: A time you shipped something under real time pressure — what did you cut, what did you keep, and what would you do differently?

Part 2 — Mock behavioral interview (20 minutes)
After writing your four stories, paste the mock interviewer prompt (from the Claude prompt below) into Claude.ai. The interviewer will ask you all four questions in sequence. Answer each question by adapting the story you prepared — but respond as if it is a real interview (no notes, no re-reading your story). After all four answers, Claude will score you.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part 1: Four labelled STAR stories, each 150-200 words. Label each: Story 1 / Story 2 / Story 3 / Story 4. Part 2: Your four mock interview answers pasted in full, followed by Claude's feedback. Total: aim for 800-1000 words across both parts.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior hiring manager at an Indian product company conducting the behavioral round of an APM interview.

I have prepared four STAR stories and I am now doing a full mock behavioral interview. Conduct the interview in sequence — ask one question, wait for my answer, then move to the next. Do not give feedback after each question. After I have answered all four, give me a full debrief.

I studied today:
- What behavioral interviews test: judgment, ownership, cross-functional maturity, influencing without authority
- STAR structure: Situation, Task, Action, Result — Action is most heavily weighted
- How Indian product interviewers differ from US-style prep: they probe for intellectual honesty, India-specific constraints, and real ownership vs team credit

The four questions you will ask, in order:

Question 1: Tell me about a time you had to influence a key decision without having direct authority over the people involved. What was the situation and how did you approach it?

Question 2: Describe a time you used data to make a product decision that someone senior to you or on your team disagreed with. How did you handle the disagreement?

Question 3: Tell me about a significant conflict you had with a designer or engineer. What was the root of the disagreement, what did you do to resolve it, and what was the outcome for the product and the working relationship?

Question 4: Walk me through a time you shipped a product or feature under serious time pressure. What did you cut, what did you keep, and in hindsight what would you do differently?

After I answer all four questions, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:

STORY 1 FEEDBACK:
- Structure (was STAR clear?): [comment]
- Specificity (did I say 'we' too much? was the action specific?): [comment]
- Outcome (quantified or clearly stated?): [comment]
- Competency demonstrated (Stakeholder Management / Team Leadership / Managing Up): [which competency and whether it was demonstrated clearly]

STORY 2 FEEDBACK: [same structure]
STORY 3 FEEDBACK: [same structure]
STORY 4 FEEDBACK: [same structure]

OVERALL DEBRIEF:
STRONGEST STORY: [which one and why]
WEAKEST STORY: [which one and what to fix]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about how these stories would land with an Indian APM interviewer at Razorpay, CRED, or Flipkart specifically]
HIRING RECOMMENDATION: [Would hire / Borderline / Would not hire at this stage] with one sentence justification

SCORE: [X/10 across all four stories combined]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Stakeholder Management — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]; Managing Up — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]; Team Leadership — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

Begin the interview now. Ask Question 1.
